title: 2025/06/17 FD
displayTitle: 2025-06-17 - Foreign Affairs, Defence and Trade Committee - 2025/26 Estimates for Appropriations within Vote Business, Science and Innovation related to New Zealand Trade and Enterprise
committee: Foreign Affairs, Defence and Trade Committee
topic: 2025/26 Estimates for Appropriations within Vote Business, Science and Innovation related to New Zealand Trade and Enterprise
description: Tuesday 17 June 2025 | Foreign Affairs, Defence and Trade Committee 2025/26 Estimates for Appropriations within Vote Business, Science and Innovation related to New Zealand Trade and Enterprise Hon Nicola Willis, Minister for Economic Growth Hon Todd McClay, Minister for Trade and Investment
descriptionSummary: 2025/26 Estimates for Appropriations within Vote Business, Science and Innovation related to New Zealand Trade and Enterprise. Hon Nicola Willis, Minister for Economic Growth. Hon Todd McClay, Minister for Trade and Investment

chunks.text: Live streaming again. Great. Thank you.

All right, we will crack into it. Good afternoon everyone. Good afternoon, ministers. Thank you very much for joining us this afternoon for having a wonderful day of scrutiny here on the Foreign Affairs Defense and Trade Committee, and look forward to your contribution over the next hour as we scrutinize the appropriations under our business side and as they relate to New Zealand's greater enterprise.

So wonderful to have you along with us, Willis, and capacity as Minister, economic growth, uh, and also is paid an investment. Thank you very much for being here. We will hand over to you for some introductory comments, and then I'm sure any questions for members? Great. Thank you Mr. Chair, for the opportunity. Um, I intend to make a couple of minutes remarks before handing over to Minister McLay, who's obviously the lead in this area, although we are joint shareholding ministers when it comes to NZTE, we are joined today by Carolyn, the head of ndi,

uh, as well as Peter Crisp, the head of NZTE. Uh, and I know that their work is familiar to members around the committee table. And while we may have our political debates, I think we are all shared in a perspective that, um, getting our exporters into the best position possible so that they can go out and win customers and markets around the world is good for New Zealand as a whole.

Um, as Minister for Economic Growth, uh, my mission is to work with ministers across government to identify those barriers that may be preventing our firms and industries and sectors from maximizing their growth possibilities, uh, in terms of job creation, taking on new markets, taking on new investments.

It is also to work out what enabling factors the government can accelerate to, um, drive that growth further in that regard. Earlier this year, we released the going for growth document, which outlined 87 actions we have underway to, uh, support growth in the economy, a critical pillar of which is promoting global trade and investment.

And that is essentially NZ t's, um, major mission. Um, it's, uh, a knowledge to the people around this table that we are in the midst of an export led recovery. Uh, it's no exaggeration, I don't think to say that our exporters, in particular, our primary producers, are driving economic growth back into this economy, and we are very grateful for their efforts.

Um, I, I see NZ T's role, uh, as twofold on, on the one hand, um, they have been very instrumental in recent months and responding to, uh, our exporters who are seeking information and assurance about what changes in tariffs globally mean for them and how it may impact their market access.

Uh, NZTE, in addition to running seminars and providing information to those who request it, have worked closely with a focus group of around a thousand exporters with whom they have very close relationships. That role of providing information and clarity to exporters and providing fraudulent government is a really crucial activity for NZTE.

The other, um, obviously very important role that they play is supporting the government's ambitious target to double the value of New Zealand's exports in 10 years, from 95 billion to 190 billion by 2034. We know that businesses have the drive and ambition needed to scale and grow internationally, uh, but there are longstanding challenges which need to be overcome in many cases for them to realize those ambitions.

So NZTE plays a key role in helping our businesses connect to the world. Uh, they succeed, uh, both by taking part in trade missions and connecting, uh, businesses to market opportunities, providing them information and supporting a range of growth initiatives, including in the documents that they have provided to you.

Uh, hundreds of international growth outcomes, uh, dozens of specific business deals that they have played a part in, in bringing on, um, Mr. McClay will detail for you further how we are now going to have an invest New Zealand entity, whose role will be to really focus how, on how we can draw international capital to bear and investing in businesses, uh, at home, and providing an easier portal into New Zealand investment for offshore investors who see prospects in our economy.

I'm gonna leave it there. Um, but, but I would, I would just say that I think it's known to New Zealanders that international trade is critical to our success. We're not gonna get rich selling for ourselves. We need to sell to the world, um, in a time of real uncertainty. It's become fashionable to concentrate on all of the things that have got harder.

Um, the story that I'm increasingly telling New Zealand exporters is that when things are hard, there is also opportunity. And I have been heartened to hear stories including, uh, from Silver Fern Farms more recently, that in the midst of global instability, there are customers looking for longer term contracts, higher value contracts, who look to New Zealand as a certain reliable partner with whom to trade in a world that is increasingly volatile.

So the role that NZTE can play in smoothing those relationships, building those relationships is significant, and we are very grateful for their work. I'll hand over to you, minister cle. Thank you so much, colleagues. Uh, pleasure to be here and, uh, to talk a bit about the work that NCT does, but I'll broaden out slightly because ultimately it's just about exports. And I think everyone here knows we're an export nation.

I often joke, uh, when I'm overseas, that we had to consume all of the food and fiber we produce. We would be larger people for it, and the world would be a, a worse off place because of the very high quality of what our farmers produce. But trade is diversified. It's not just the primary sector anymore. That indeed it is about innovation and agri agritech, but it's across the board from services to other technologies.

Uh, actually, we now, with our trade agreements, a significant network of trade agreements around the world. Uh, Kiwis Punch, uh, well above their weight New Zealand trade and enterprise job is to assist them, not to export for them, to help them, to help them export more, to do more and markets, uh, uh, uh, where they are entering. Uh, largely the very large companies fend for themselves, rightly so.

So it's about where we can add value. Uh, NZT over the last year, uh, supported around 6,000 export companies, um, helped about just over 1500 of those, uh, export deals, uh, worth, uh, $3.8 billion. So that's from a, a small amount of engagement helped to very, very hands-on, depending upon the company.

International growth outcomes increased from two and a half thousand to 2,700, uh, activities during that period of time. And we're particularly active in, uh, with Maori exporters helping unlock $126 billion worth of their asset base. We've got a dedicated team there that focus on that because it's a very important part of the export, uh, economy.

Uh, just, uh, figures. You will have had them, but I'll go over them very quickly. Uh, the 2025 budget is, uh, $159 million. That's, uh, down on last year. Predominantly, that is an uplift of, uh, um, $11 million that is, uh, spent on investment and it will move over to invest New Zealand when that is created past, following the, the passing of legislation, a number of other small changes that flow out of last year around international growth fund, which is really recalibrating it to, to, to what can be spent and how we more effectively can deliver for the taxpayer

and the exporter. Um, uh, around the International Growth Fund, it does play a very important role, but we've had a focus on making sure that the money is well spent and actually it is achieving. Uh, we want to help companies do things they might not have done otherwise, or to do them more quickly rather than just, you know, providing them taxpayer resource to do what they might have done anyway.

Uh, 63 IGF expansion projects, uh, have generated $238 million against a $41 million investment. Some of that funding is over a number of years, uh, uh, 127 commercial deals, uh, uh, worth $270 million as a result of that. And, and, uh, Pete might be able to speak more to that, should he wish.

Uh, final two bits I wanna touch on is around the creation of Invest New Zealand. Uh, um, I think Damien, during the debate, sort of ask questions about why not leave it there. Ultimately, we want to focus on investment and a role it can play in growing the economy. So if you think about Sir Peter Blackman's report, uh, where he was talking about sort of the Tech and Science Center, he said, actually, more investment needs to be driven into that, and therefore changes are needed.

And so, uh, invest New Zealand will work within the rules. They don't go out and decide what it is they want to do. They don't have any responsibility for investment policy or the Overseas Investment Act. They will work within those rules. And, uh, we've been going through a process since announcement of looking at where they can best add value and grow investment into parts of the economy that will benefit New Zealanders the most.

Uh, I've had a small team of, uh, people that have been offering advice for free, uh, from the private sector experts that have worked in a number of these fields with less advice, more, you know, talking about how this might work best. And we're moving the stage when the legislation has passed, we'll have to have an uplift of, uh, staff who are involved in the investment component of New Zealand trade and Enterprise to come across so that those investment deals that be working on continue.

And then I go through the process of pointing a, uh, permanent board, and they'll go to A CEO so that we can focus on, uh, you know, how to get the most from, uh, this taxpayer investment. Uh, the budget that they will have is, uh, just over $21 million a year. $11 million comes from NZTE and industrial $10 million that comes out of the science, innovation, and tech, uh, portfolio.

And that's so that we can focus particularly on how we bring investment into growing that very important part of our economy that actually flows through to much more, uh, uh, export. The final thing is around tariffs and the great uncertainty. I think the committee had the foreign minister here earlier, and you might have touched upon this, a New Zealand trade enterprise along with, uh, the, the Ministry of Foreign Affairs and Trade has been working with, uh, customers, exporters that are involved in the US market since the 10% tariff was

put and put in place both directly with them as well as running cinema seminars or online events to pro provide them with information and talk to 'em about the option that they have. Uh, nobody in New Zealand thinks tariffs are a good thing, and so we'd be very, uh, clear in our view of this. But in the meantime, until we can create more certainty, either in our relationship with the US or in export markets around the world, working closely with companies that might be affected, uh, particularly the small to medium sized ones, so

that they can see what their options are. And with that, uh, colleagues, happy to, to collect your questions. Excellent. Thanks, ministers for Those comments. Uh, first Question on a Royal Damien O'Connor, look, thank you very much and, and welcome to committee. And, um, I, I congratulate you on the proposition. I guess it's, it's, um, uh, one of, uh, 11% cut in expenditure to deliver highest the amount of exports, uh, which is a kind of a proposition most people would find challenging.

So I guess the question is, where will the double exports come from if we are cutting back the efforts of what is the number one yeah. Government agency to drive that both enterprise and trade? Well, well, I think what you've done is you have mistaken money that government spends for more exports.

And so, you know, using that logic, we should triple and double the budget, uh, you know, rather than building schools or hospitals with the hope that it would work. What we've been doing across the board is being much more focused on outcomes. And so actually we've seen the work that, uh, New Zealand Trade Enterprise has done over the last year, increase on previous years, uh, with the same amount or a lower amount of money.

However, having said that, you'll have seen over the period of time you were in government, uh, money was given, uh, to NZTE as part of the COVID fund. It was always coming out of the system in the early days last year, some of that was there. We've made some other changes, but actually, uh, the ability for the workforce to deliver more than they were previously, I think is in the stats that I mentioned.

So, so can I just ask if NCT, I guess if we have Invest New Zealand, which is the proposition, we can go back to that after, and, uh, the Reach provincial growth fund or the money coming to help economic development agencies and, and their initiatives, uh, what job does that leave for NZTE? Well, I, I can answer that then perhaps, uh, because it's more of a political question, so I wouldn't give that to Pete.

Mm-hmm. So largely, I guess, uh, it is what is in its name. And so what we are doing is working with New Zealand Trade and Enterprise and how we can help them help New Zealanders export more and open up, open up opportunities in those markets. We are asking Invest New Zealand to, uh, find investment from around the world that is good for New Zealanders and good for the New Zealand economy and to help us grow the economy and opportunities for all New Zealanders, uh, within that.

But I mean, if you put the investment aside, uh, uh, you know, with the funding that we have available through the, uh, the, um, the, um, the IGF, that's, uh, that's only part of what New Zealand trade and Enterprise does. To give you an example, the, um, free trade agreements in the UK that I got to announce was on its way.

You got to negotiate and do, or the EU one, uh, which we started finished, and, and then I was able to get it in force, has delivered, uh, uh, more, you know, growth in exports during that period of time than many other agreements. 20% increase in exports of the uk, 28% in one year for the eu. And New Zealand trade enterprise has been doing really important things in that market in all of them with the resource they had, in fact, much more as a result of better access for Kiwis.

We've asked them not to, uh, help those that are able to achieve this by themselves, but some of those small to medium sized companies that might be in markets to look at other markets in the eu, that they can have advantage as a result of the trade agreement and more certainty or companies that should be there that might be exporting elsewhere. I think about it in a really simple way, which is NZ t's role is to look at New Zealand exporters and to say, how can we help you grow your export opportunities offshore and ensure that you are maximizing our free trade agreements

that are in place and building on those, the role of investment New Zealand will be, rather than to face into New Zealand to face the world and say, how can we make the most of the international capital that is looking for a home and provided a funnel back into investment in the New Zealand economy? Because we know there are people who want to invest here, uh, but they won't always have the expertise or knowledge to invest in individual firms or entities.

And we'll be looking for broader vehicles by which to do that. So one faces the world, one faces New Zealand, both jobs are critically important, and both jobs are critically supported by the work of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs and Trade who provide the enabling architecture, which we require in order to advance investment and trade. The legislation has gone through the house or in the process.

Um, and so it's very hard to pick from that or any the, um, the risk, uh, the criteria that will govern or how, how decisions will be made around what is high value and what is low value investment. Yeah. So can you clarify the area? Is it, will it be into land? Uh, will it be into housing, uh, commercial property or what other areas?

Yeah, specific areas of the economy will, So, so I can help with that. The reason that the, uh, legislation doesn't go into that degree of detail is because it's for current future governments to help create direction. Firstly. Secondly, it doesn't really matter what the government wants investment in. If the country's not really, investors don't wanna put money in there, it's, it's not gonna happen. We don't have the ability to force foreigns to come and invest in New Zealand.

We've gotta make sure the settings are correct. But thirdly, the legislation is very similar to that of New Zealand Trade Enterprise was first set up, so it's the establishment of what it should do, it's competencies, and then we go from there. So to answer your questions, uh, uh, I mean, ultimately there are a number of things that New Zealand chain enterprise have been looking at as a result of ministerial direction, uh, previously, you know, around investment.

And there will be priorities for the government and we'll get to set those out and do that publicly. But all of the areas that you are raised particularly on property, I, IIII wanna say that I, I get that, you know, uh, we think when we're in opposition, if we jump on the property bandwagon, we scare Kiwis, but ultimately invest New Zealand will work under the rules that are in place to allow investment.

And so whatever they may be currently in the future under a current or future government around those issues you've raised, they will have to meet the requirements of that legislation. But I will give you one example. So if you think about the tech space and startups, we have lots of great ideas in New Zealand companies that came from, you know, just an idea down in Inver Cargo, the become rocket rocket lab that all of New Zealand should know about and be very proud of because of the significant achievement that that,

that they have made, particularly in bringing other investors to come into, you know, high tech and deep tech. There isn't enough, uh, funding available within New Zealand. One of the opportunities and invest New Zealand will have is to go work with the private sector, perhaps to set up a fund, not for them to manage. It could be managed in the private sector and to attract investment from banks and others around the world.

That sole job is to focus on startups that have a greater likelihood, perhaps, of not succeeding without, uh, without uh, uh, um, you know, without capital and grow those as part of the economy. And so we'll have opportunities, um, post third reading when the legislation's in place, and we start establishing this committee and elsewhere about exactly what it might achieve.

But I would say to you, they will only be focusing on investment that's good for New Zealand and good for New Zealanders. And so you can still vote for it in third reading. Damien, just very briefly, the two Things. Do you know how much we New Zealanders and New Zealand invests offshore? Most New Zealand invests offshore. Yeah, that Is capital that we have that we vest else.

Yeah, I, I mean, I've had it, but um, ultimately my focus is on what investment comes to us. And the reason for that is, um, I'm sure one of our wonderful professionals can do a quick Google on that. Give us the Number, but I guess, but I, I, I guess the point is there are two things we could do. We could say we don't want foreign direct investment from anywhere around the world, and New Zealand has never actually done that, although we've created settings and we can say we're gonna tell New Zealanders through the super fund everything else, what

to invest in in New Zealand. 'cause I suspect that's where this question is going. Yes, you are right. New Zealanders should invest in the New Zealand economy. There have been many reasons the past have chosen not to, but ultimately we have to get the investment setting rights not only for New Zealand investors as well as, um, you know, uh, uh, investors from overseas invest in New Zealand's job.

Uh, uh, it's not a policy setting organization. It's a found foreigners to bring investment to New Zealand. If it's good for New Zealand, good for New Zealanders, we should be welcoming. Yeah, There's a, there's a simple, there's a simple kinda lineup of facts that I look at, which is one, um, we know that one of the factors that underlies New Zealand's lower rates of productivity, it's not that our workers don't work big hours or aren't smart people.

They're all of those things, but they have lower rates of capital intensity in their firms. Therefore they're reliant on less, uh, less good technology that lines up with lower rates of capital investment when you compare us, us our economy to other economies. Mm-hmm. And then that lines up with more restrictive foreign investment settings than in many of the countries we would like to compare ourselves with.

It is easy to join the dots between those points and see that if we were better at funneling international capital into our firm's sectors and industries, we would get higher rates of productivity and growth. And there is every reason for investors around the world to want to expose themselves to the New Zealand economy and invest in it. But right now we make it pretty hard for them to do it.

If you're an investor who said, look, I want to get invested in New Zealand's food production industries, that looks pretty good. Well, where do you start? And so invest New Zealand is our answer to that question. One Final, can I just question if I can back up the comment? I think if you look at small advanced economies, uh, we've actually got quite low levels of integration with the world.

So we're only 25% exports in GDP. That's a very low number amongst the advanced export economies. The level of FDI, uh, is relatively low. We have about a hundred billion dollar FDI stock. But ask your question with one of the lowest countries in the world for ODI overseas direct investment.

So because of our scale and distance, we've actually got a relatively low, uh, economic integration with the rest of the world. And that's probably one of the things that drives our lower productivity. I, I've seen those figures. I just need, the question does, does ownership matter? Uh, well go a bit further 'cause that's fairly open question because you know, if I think about your car, it supported you own it, what do you mean? You've got, I guess, the question Ownership One Owner To the owner business supplementary.

And then, um, thank you minister. I just wanna come back to that original question that we, we've sort of drifted into invest here, but the original question was in the context of the, of the budget this year about that, that that goal of doubling the value of our trade exports. And certainly when I speak to exporters like I did last week, uh, exporter exporting high-end audio equipment, I visited them in Dan Kirkpatrick's electorate, really excited by that.

They want to see that happen. I think the question is, can you, can you outline for us in pretty clear terms two things. One, what is the government doing towards that goal? Yeah. And secondly, I guess we're year in now, so how is progress going? Yeah, 12 months down track. So look, a couple of things. I think it's really important to set a target, a very ambitious goal.

And it is ambitious because it focuses the mind of government and agencies, but also the private sector. Uh, I'll come in a moment to some of the things we've been doing in the first year, uh, um, around that. But I think what's been, uh, most encouraging is the private sector across the board, individual companies or sector grouping, stepping up and talking to us about what they're doing to help meet the ambitious goal of doubling exports by value.

Over 10 years, I was with a, a group of the forestry sector forest owners, uh, last week on the side of the field days, came out of the field days to talk to them at one of their annual meetings. And two of them said, this is what we are doing to help double X export by value over 10 years. And so it is ambitious and I wanna recognize that, um, uh, I think it was last year in event I was speaking at Chris Kin mentioned that it was something that was important and the Labor Party would join that.

So let's be ambitious, no issue with that. So we have said there's another ways to do that. We need better trade access, uh, no doubt at all. Uh, you know, our FDA network, we have, uh, around a hundred free trade agreements, uh, investment agreements or double tax agreements with countries of the world. We need more of those to level the playing field and give certainty.

And that's an and strategy we want to sell to this country and that one and the others and so on. You know, the, the latest one to enter into force with the eu FDA, an extra billion dollars worth of exports last year, that wouldn't have happened otherwise, predominantly in the primary sector, but not only there's a lot of tech in there as well, and that's just money in the pockets of Kiwis. Secondly, we said we wanna do more trade missions than, uh, than has done before and a term of parliament, and we remain on track for that.

I think the number we have to achieve is 21. We are thinking more about the quality of them than the volume of them. But what we're also doing is measuring the deals that are done as a result of that mission. Some of which are just done because a minister or prime minister is there. Others, because, you know, they work towards them. Uh, and, uh, Pete, I think it was about $630 million worth of deals have been signed up during the trade missions over the last year and a a bit, which is quite, uh, significant.

The third thing we have to do is deal with non tariff barriers. And, and, um, this is, this is a very important area because we can do every trade deal in the world and get the tariff rate to zero everywhere. But if the standards are such that we can't sell into these markets, or there is a great cost imposed upon New Zealand exporters, then we won't get near, uh, achieving what we need to.

I'll give you, uh, two examples of this that I think are very important. One, which we have partially resolved and continue to work on. The other needs a lot of work. Uh, the first is around we use the forestry sector. Again, log exports to India because the decision of New Zealand government to change, uh, the use of methyl bromide within New Zealand, India wouldn't accept our logs anymore.

It was some $300 million worth of trade from memory. The reason logs go there rather than process timber, as the tariff rate on process timber makes it prohibitive, whereas logs is lower. That's often the case. People want to take the logs and add value, and the more value you add to that piece of wood, the log, you get tariff escalation.

So it restrictions New Zealand. There's, uh, uh, from exporting, you know, value added product, we're able to negotiate with India, uh, a a, a change to their regime, something they hadn't done before. So the logs trade can start again. Uh, it is on a type of trial basis as we look to have them accept other products that, that we use to China and elsewhere, and we think we will get there, but it's gone from almost no trade to not quite a hundred million dollars I'm told or forecast over a period of time of a year or so.

The second one that we need to crack, because this is about value over volume. So at the moment, New Zealand structural timber manufacturers can't sell timber into Australia because their standards are different. They say our wood grows too quickly, it's too soft. That's a market of some $300 million and more I advised.

And Australia has a structural timber deficit, they must import from the world. So I've engaged, uh, with NZ t's help and MPI and, and and Ministry of Foreign Affairs with Australia on that, both their agriculture and forestry minister and trade minister. And they're undertaking a review, another, uh, scientific evaluation of our wood.

If we can get that right, then our sales to Australia go through the roof of process trimbo, and there's more reason for people to add value in the regions and build sawmill to, to hit there. Yeah. So the final point of that is Australia's either right or we, right. We can't both because of that, right? Our houses are falling down, which they're not. So that non tariff barrier alone will give certainty to, uh, an industry, uh, the far industry that desperately needs them.

Alright, Dr. Lawrence. Nan, thank you Chair. Uh, I have two supplementary questions on this, and, and one of them is on, because we're talking about investing New Zealand Zealand before now as part of the regulatory impact segment to the bill, the budget that was sought in budget 2025 was $155 million. Yeah. Budget 2025 is $84.6 million. Mm-hmm.

That is at the reduction of 45%. It's not just 10%. Like what they on Con was mentioning. What, how are you intending on achieving the outcome or purpose of investment in New Zealand, considering that what they're asking for and what they received a substantially much less? Well, um, uh, speaking as the Minister of Economic growth, uh, every initiative that goes through the budget process has to be negotiated with the Minister of Finance.

And, uh, the experience that we have with her, uh, is that she's very demanding about can you demonstrate value for money and impact for the dollars that you are asking for? Because every dollar has multiple purposes. The first purpose that we should always have is a sacrosanct purpose is that it could do a lot of good within a New Zealand household, particularly New Zealanders, who are suffering with a very high cost of living.

Uh, and so we need to ask ourselves, are we getting the best impact when it comes to invest New Zealand? Yes. Um, there was always the potential to make this bigger from day one. Instead, the approach that we've taken as a government is to say, let's fund it to establish itself, to demonstrate that it is able to achieve the things we wish it to achieve and to get impact for those dollars.

Once that's up and running, we can then consider further business cases for expanding it. I think this is a prudent balance of making sure we are getting impact before we put, send too many dollars chasing after the objective. So we'd be expecting some tangible performance measures, which currently doesn't exist in budget. 2025 Documents. Yes, absolutely. Because that will of course be one of the first jobs of the incoming board to set those performance measures so that we can hold them accountable.

Okay. Second question. In terms of the export growth, um, minister you mentioned before in terms of the fact that we're signing up to new free trade agreements as well as comprehensive economic partnership agreements. Um, what is the proportion then, currently we're trending in terms of our proportion of export, sorry, our export in proportion to GDP, Uh, export is a proportion of GDP, uh, I know from the primary sector, which is about growing to 13%.

Can someone help me with that please? It's about 25% exports to GDP, um, and it's been between, between 25% and 30% over the last 15 years. So it's a, it's gone up and down a little bit, but proportionately, uh, across success of governments, the size of the export sector relative to the total economy hasn't changed Much. And more recently, we've had a bumper time for goods exports, some of the highest, um, numbers on a volume and value basis, uh, on record.

Um, some of the best proportions there. Uh, and you will have seen in the Primary Industries report released at field days last week. Yeah. Uh, that we are now predicting bumper, uh, volumes and values across a range of agricultural sectors. So the, the trends are looking good. It'll Be good for us to get a trend because the question then is if we are signing up to more FTEs and CPAs, but then we're actually seeing that it's stabilizing, it's always gonna be roughly the same.

What's the point for us to spend all of this money with and the t to actually sign? Yeah. Well, well, well, it's, it's rarely something that New Zealand has done since the United Kingdom joined. What was then the common market has looked to diversify. It's not for the government necessarily to, uh, uh, say to exporters where they should go. Although with New Zealand trained and enterprise, we support and we direct where there are opportunities, uh, um, exporters have to decide upon risk for themselves.

And they do that very, very well for a range of a range of reasons. So the reason that we have and want more trade agreements is not necessarily because trade will increase, although it does, uh, you know, uh, because it may not increase as a percentage of DDP because, you know, the, the, the country's economy grows in many areas other than just export.

But the reason we want this as a trade agreement does nothing more than gives rights and obligations and sets very clear rules for business to be able to rely upon, uh, uh, with Australia. Uh, we have absolute certainty if you want to export to Australia how you'll be treated and what your rights are in that market and vice versa. And so ultimately we continue to negotiate trade deals that are of high quality to bring costs down through tariff rates, but to give certainty to exporters so that they can diversify.

And I think, you know, across the board, not just the primary sector, we've done that very well. The final point I wanted to make around this is we do talk about goods exports a lot because we are one of the most efficient food and fiber producers in the world, the highest quality and safest on the back of our farmers and forests and fishers. Uh, but that's only part of the equation where the real opportunity now into the future is, uh, is around, uh, that of both services and innovation.

So if you, uh, uh, a young New Zealander that chooses to study and have a career in a tech space, you will earn more, um, by magnitudes than other parts of the economy.
